perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not 2 specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any employee of the Company; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the qualifiers such as "director, officer or other senior or key employee" from the non-solicitation clause, the agreement now broadly prohibits employing or soliciting 'any employee.' This contradicts the principle of employee freedom to seek employment as outlined in various state laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified text, by broadly prohibiting the solicitation and hiring of 'any employee,' could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint on trade, conflicting with California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the non-solicitation clause to include 'any employee' of the company, removing the original qualifiers that limited the restriction to directors, officers, senior, or key employees. This change contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By broadly prohibiting the solicitation and hiring of any employee, the modified text creates an unlawful restraint on trade, preventing employees from seeking employment elsewhere. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states this, and Snippet 2 reiterates the same point. The original text was more narrowly tailored to protect key personnel, while the modified text places a much wider restriction on the employees, violating the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will have the exclusive authority to decide what Confidential Information (if any) is to be made available to the Receiving Party and its Representatives. Neither the Company nor any of its directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, advisors, attorneys, accountants and affiliates (collectively, the “ Company Representatives ”) will be under any obligation to make any particular Confidential Information available to the Receiving Party or any of the Receiving Party’s Representatives or to supplement or update any Confidential Information previously furnished. Neither the Company nor any of the Company Representatives has made or is making any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information, and neither the Company nor any of the Company Representatives will have any liability to the Receiving Party or to any of the Receiving Party’s Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of any Confidential Information or any inaccuracies or errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will have the exclusive authority to decide what Confidential Information (if any) is to be made available to the Receiving Party and its Representatives. Neither the Company nor any of its directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, advisors, attorneys, accountants and affiliates (collectively, the “ Company Representatives ”) will be under any obligation to make any particular Confidential Information available to the Receiving Party or any of the Receiving Party’s Representatives or to supplement or update any Confidential Information previously furnished. Neither the Company nor any of the Company Representatives has made or is making any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information, except for representations regarding material omissions, and neither the Company nor any of the Company Representatives will have any liability to the Receiving Party or to any of the Receiving Party’s Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of any Confidential Information or any inaccuracies or errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "except for representations regarding material omissions," the contract creates an ambiguous duty of disclosure. While the original text disclaims all representations and warranties, the modification implies a responsibility to avoid material omissions, which is in conflict with the clause disclaiming any liability for omissions. This could be interpreted as a violation of SEC Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 10b-5 prohibits making untrue statements of a material fact or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading. By including 'except for representations regarding material omissions' while simultaneously disclaiming liability for omissions, this change creates an ambiguity and potential liability which might be seen as a violation of Rule 10b-5 depending on the specific information provided.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception ("except for representations regarding material omissions") to a prior disclaimer of all warranties regarding accuracy and completeness of confidential information. This creates a contradiction because the original text states there is no liability for omissions, while the modified text implies liability for 'material omissions'. This directly contradicts SEC Rule 10b-5, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, makes it unlawful to 'omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made... not misleading'. The altered text creates an ambiguity as to the company's responsibility regarding material omissions, potentially conflicting with the original disclaimer of liability for any omissions and thus establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, the Receiving Party will attempt to return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information may be destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Promptly' to 'attempt' and 'shall be destroyed' to 'may be destroyed' weakens the obligation to return and destroy confidential information. This weakening conflicts with the intent of confidentiality agreements and laws protecting trade secrets such as the Defend Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to return and destroy confidential information, the modification increases the risk of misappropriation and weakens the protections afforded by the DTSA, making it more difficult for the Company to seek legal recourse if the Receiving Party fails to adequately protect its trade secrets.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall be destroyed' to 'may be destroyed' weakens the legal obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The original text mandates destruction, while the modified text makes it optional, increasing the risk of misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Civil proceedings' related to 'protection of trade secrets' under 18 USC 1836, indicating legal recourse for trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the destruction obligation, the modified text potentially undermines the legal protection afforded by the DTSA, as the receiving party might not adequately protect the company's trade secrets, and this can be challenged in civil proceedings.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally specifies New York law as the governing law. By changing this to Delaware law, where both companies are incorporated, it introduces uncertainty because New York has significant nexus to the agreement due to the Receiving Party's principal place of business. This could create issues in contract interpretation under the principles of conflict of laws, particularly if the outcome differs significantly between the two states.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law of the state chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied if the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or there is any other reasonable basis for the parties' choice. Changing the governing law from New York to Delaware could be challenged if it appears the sole purpose was to avoid specific New York laws, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies New York law, while the changed contract specifies Delaware law. The Law Explanation states that changing the governing law could be challenged if the sole purpose was to avoid specific laws, creating uncertainty in enforcement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions connected to a case, where the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating New York has a significant nexus to the agreement due to the Receiving Party's principal place of business, which introduces uncertainty by changing the governing law. The change from New York to Delaware could be seen as an attempt to circumvent specific New York laws, thereby creating a legal contradiction according to the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, as explained.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the parties hereto with respect to the matters contained herein, and may be amended, modified or waived only by a separate writing executed by the Receiving Party and the Company expressly so amending, modifying or waiving this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the parties hereto with respect to the matters contained herein, and may be amended, modified or waived by mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "only by a separate writing executed by the Receiving Party and the Company expressly so amending, modifying or waiving this Agreement," the amended text now allows for implied or verbal amendments to the agreement. This contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which in most states, requires certain contracts, including those lasting over a year (this one is for 18 months), to be in writing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Frauds, General Obligations Law § 5-701
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 requires certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. By allowing amendments, modifications, or waivers without a written agreement, the altered language violates the Statute of Frauds, rendering such modifications potentially unenforceable.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-701, also known as the Statute of Frauds. The original text required amendments to be in writing and executed by both parties. The changed text allows for amendments by 'mutual agreement,' which could include verbal agreements. NY GOB § 5-701(a) states that 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith...if such agreement...By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof...' Since the original contract lasted for 18 months, any modifications would also need to be in writing to be enforceable under the Statute of Frauds, as per the scraped snippets. By removing the requirement for written modifications, the changed text allows for potentially unenforceable verbal modifications, creating a legal contradiction.
